column15: Here, I would diverge in what I could advise this person depending on what I feel they would be ready for. Ultimately the "most important" thing is that all feelings pass. This will just be a blip in their life that won't seem so important down the line. But I think this would be too existential and not useful for most people, so I would probably tell them to focus on their own objectives. What are they trying to accomplish? Ask them questions about is it most important to do the work itself or have recognition from one particular person. Actually, it seem like I would more try to help them figure out what is important for them rather than impose what I think is important.
column16: I would tell them to look inside themselves and try to feel what's right for them. But I also understand that some people don't really have that capacity. But I would still encourage them, and maybe give them concrete tips, like breathe, journal, body-scan. It's ok to consider the views of others, but ultimately if one does it at the expense of their own inner sense of direction, it becomes inefficient energetically and may have costs later on their health and well-being.
column17: I would tell them to focus on why the thing was important to them, other than the result, what mattered to them in the first place. Acceptance that sometimes things don't go your way, but take a step back, not get lost in negative emotions especially those related to who you "should" be as a person.
column18: Enjoy it! And spread the joy. Don't make it all about yourself. Try to recognize what it meant for everyone on the team and use it to help them and the team as a whole grow. And it's always nice to have good news - stay in the moment and enjoy it.
column19: Does the person have leverage within the group? If so, I think it's worth exploring if it's possible to change the group's decision. Or trying to exit the group. But sometimes you can't really... for instance, when your country elects someone who is doing things you really don't agree with. I think keeping oneself in a state of outrage isn't really the best way to spend your energy. And at the end of the day, you are not your group. Taking some distance, and focusing on one's own mental health, and focusing on where you do have leverage.
column20: I'm curious about the way sacrifice is defined in this question. Often times people think of sacrifice as putting other's needs above your own. For me this is a view "from the outside." When putting others' needs above your own, you can either do it in a way where you feel aligned or where you feel like you have to but you don't really want to. The way the question is framed, there's no access to the internal state of the person making the sacrifice, so the only thing you can really think about what you're seeing is "someone is doing something that's putting others' needs above their own" (provided you can even know what someone's needs are deep down). Even calling it a sacrifice imposes an interpretation.
column21: It means what it means to them. Change is part of life. Some people might have an easier time accepting it than others.
column22: Ha, I'd like to say, great opportunity to update your beliefs! Because learning to adapt is a fundamental part of being human. But I understand that this can be hard for people as many people tend to be quite emotionally attached to their belief systems.
